skill.name: mcp-builder
skill.description: Guide for designing, building, testing, and evaluating MCP servers in TypeScript or Python with practical SDK patterns.
skill.summary: Build well-structured MCP servers with transport, tool design, testing, and evaluation guidance.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many severe-looking hits, but review shows most are Markdown examples, MCP terminology, placeholder URLs, and normal SDK guidance. The real risks are contextual: the optional evaluation harness can run user-selected stdio commands, connect to remote MCP endpoints, and pass credentials supplied by the user. No prompt injection, obfuscation, hardcoded live secret, or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: User-selected MCP server command execution
medium_findings.description: The evaluation harness can start a stdio MCP server from a command and arguments supplied by the user. This is expected for testing local MCP servers, but it can execute untrusted code and pass environment variables to that process.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The harness explicitly accepts a command, args, and env values for stdio transport and passes them into the MCP stdio connection. The behavior is intentional for an evaluator, so the risk is contextual rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: User-supplied remote MCP endpoint connections
medium_findings.description: The evaluation tooling supports SSE and HTTP connections to user-provided URLs with optional headers. This is legitimate MCP test functionality, but users could expose bearer tokens or connect to untrusted endpoints if they supply unsafe values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly creates remote MCP clients from caller-provided URLs and headers. The examples use placeholder endpoints, so there is no evidence of hardcoded exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Credential handling appears in examples and evaluator inputs
low_findings.description: The skill documents API keys, tokens, and environment variable use for MCP testing and generated servers. The examples are placeholders and mostly recommend environment variables, but users should avoid passing real secrets to untrusted servers or remote endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references show placeholder API keys and token-passing patterns, not live secrets. The concern remains because the evaluator and generated servers are designed to work with real credentials.

low_findings.title: Most static analyzer hits are documentation false positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick and weak cryptography findings occur in Markdown guides and code examples, not in hidden execution logic. Reviewed content shows MCP implementation guidance, SDK examples, and security best practices rather than malicious obfuscation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are prose and illustrative snippets. I found no evidence of weak cryptographic routines, obfuscation, or Ruby shell backticks being used by the skill runtime.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess-based stdio transport
dangerous_patterns.description: The stdio connection path can run a local server command chosen by the user. This is normal for MCP stdio testing, but it should be limited to trusted server code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The stdio transport is documented as subprocess-based and implemented with caller-provided command values. This confirms external command execution, but only through explicit evaluator use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network-capable MCP and API examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches streamable HTTP MCP servers and API clients. These examples are appropriate for MCP development, but they expand the security boundary to external services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The locations show explicit WebFetch guidance and HTTP client examples. The URLs are official documentation or placeholders, so this is a capability risk, not evidence of exfiltration.

content.value_statement: MCP server projects often fail because tool design, transport choices, and evaluation are unclear. This skill provides structured guidance for planning, implementing, testing, and evaluating MCP servers in Python or TypeScript.
actual_capabilities: Explains MCP server planning, tool naming, pagination, error handling, and annotations.
Provides TypeScript and Python implementation patterns for common MCP server structures.
Shows how to choose stdio or streamable HTTP transport for local and remote servers.
Includes guidance for authentication, environment variables, and external API clients.
Provides an evaluation workflow and scripts for testing MCP servers with question-answer tasks.
Links to reference guides for MCP best practices, Python examples, TypeScript examples, and evaluations.
limitations: It is a development guide, not a ready-made MCP server for a specific service.
Generated servers still require human review for security, privacy, and API correctness.
The evaluation harness requires valid dependencies, a Claude API key, and a target MCP server.
External API behavior depends on the third-party service documentation and credentials.

use_cases.title: Create a New Service Integration
use_cases.description: Plan and implement an MCP server that exposes a product API through clear tools and validated inputs.

use_cases.title: Standardize Internal MCP Patterns
use_cases.description: Use the best-practice checklist to align naming, transport, pagination, authentication, and error handling across teams.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Tool Quality
use_cases.description: Create read-only evaluation questions and run the included harness to measure whether agents can use the server effectively.

prompt_templates.title: Plan My MCP Server
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to plan an MCP server for [service]. Identify the core tools, transport choice, authentication approach, and first implementation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design Tool Schemas
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder guidance to design MCP tools for [workflow]. Include tool names, input fields, output format, annotations, pagination, and error behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Review an MCP Server
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this MCP server using the mcp-builder quality checklist. Focus on tool descriptions, input validation, auth handling, transport setup, and response consistency.

prompt_templates.title: Build Evaluation Tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder evaluation process to create ten read-only evaluation questions for this MCP server. Make each answer stable, verifiable, and realistic.

output_examples.input: Plan an MCP server for a project management API.
output_examples.output: A build plan with transport choice, authentication notes, tool list, schema guidance, pagination rules, and a testing sequence.

output_examples.input: Review my MCP tool descriptions and annotations.
output: Clear issues grouped by tool name.
Annotation corrections for read-only, destructive, idempotent, and open-world behavior.

output_examples.input: Create evaluation questions for my server.
output_examples.output: A set of realistic read-only questions with expected answers and guidance for running the evaluation harness.

best_practices: Use narrow tool names, strict input schemas, clear output formats, and pagination for every list operation.
Store credentials outside source code and test only against trusted local servers or approved remote endpoints.
Create read-only evaluations that use stable data and verify exact answers before measuring agent performance.
anti_patterns: Do not expose broad shell, file, or network access through vague MCP tools.
Do not hardcode real API keys, bearer tokens, or private service URLs in examples or generated code.
Do not treat annotations as security controls; enforce authorization, validation, and rate limits in code.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build MCP servers that expose external services through well-designed tools for AI agents.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete server automatically?
faq.answer: It provides patterns, examples, and review steps. The final server still needs implementation and testing.

faq.question: Which languages does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers TypeScript with the MCP SDK and Python with FastMCP.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: What should I review before publishing a server?
faq.answer: Review authentication, input validation, permissions, output size, pagination, error messages, and evaluation results.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes optional scripts that can run local commands, connect to remote MCP endpoints, and pass user-supplied credentials.
